items.title: Lumbar disc herniation clinic
items.contentHtml:
Clinic of lumbar disc herniation

  • Low back pain with radicular irradiation (sciatica) is the cardinal symptom; The pain is exacerbated with Valsalva maneuvers (coughing, sneezing, defecation) due to increased intrathecal pressure.
  • Radicular compression produces paresthesias and motor deficit in the corresponding dermatome and myotome; the loss of osteotendinous reflex guides the affected root.
  • Posterolateral hernia is the most common (90% in L4-L5 and L5-S1) due to the weakness of the posterior longitudinal ligament.
  • neurogenic claudication (pain when walking that subsides when sitting) differentiates lumbar stenosis from disc herniation.
  • The examination should include Lasègue and Bragard to detect root irritation.

items.title: Lasègue sign and tension maneuvers
items.contentHtml:
Lasègue sign and tension maneuvers

  • Lasègue sign (straight leg raising maneuver): positive if it reproduces radicular pain between 30° and 70° of hip flexion with the knee extended; angles <30° suggest more proximal injury.
  • Bragard maneuver: dorsiflexion of the ankle during Lasègue increases tension on the sciatic nerve and confirms the root origin.
  • Crossed Lasègue sign: pain in the affected leg when raising the contralateral leg; It is highly specific (>90%) for herniated disc.
  • Kernig maneuver and Brudzinski are used for meningeal, not radicular, irritation; Its positivity in the context of sciatica requires ruling out epidural abscess.
  • The Lasegue maneuver has sensitivity of 80-90% but specificity of 40-60%; the crossover increases the specificity.

items.foco: Root dermatomes L4, L5 and S1
items.title: Root dermatomes L4, L5 and S1
items.contentHtml:
Dermatomas of L4, L5 and S1 roots

  • L4 root: dermatome on the anteromedial aspect of the thigh, knee and medial aspect of the leg to the medial malleolus; Its lesion affects the patellar reflex.
  • L5 Root: dermatome on the lateral aspect of the thigh, anterior aspect of the leg and dorsum of the foot up to the first toe; It does not have its own osteotendinous reflex.
  • S1 Root: dermatome on the posterior surface of the thigh, calf, heel and lateral edge of the foot; Its injury cancels the Achilles reflex.
  • The L4-L5 disc herniation compresses the L5 root; L5-S1 hernia compresses S1 root; the L3-L4 hernia compresses the L4 root.
  • The motor deficit L4: knee extension (quadriceps); L5: dorsiflexion of the first toe and foot (tibialis anterior); S1: plantar flexion (gastrocnemius).

items.title: Differential diagnosis of sciatica
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis of sciatica

  • Sacroiliac joint dysfunction: pain in the gluteal region, without dermatome, with positive provocation tests (Gaenslen, Faber).
  • Piriformis syndrome: pain in the buttock and posterior thigh due to compression of the sciatic nerve; worsens with hip internal rotation; Lasègue can be positive but without neurological deficit.
  • Lumbar canal stenosis: neurogenic claudication, relief when sitting, multiple roots affected; MRI shows decreased canal diameter.
  • Vascular pathology: vascular claudication (decreased pulses, relief when stopping without having to sit down) and absence of neurological signs.
  • Spinal tumor or infection: night pain, weight loss, fever, risk factors; MRI with contrast is diagnostic.

items.title: Indications for urgent MRI
items.contentHtml:
Indications for urgent MRI

  • Caquina equina syndrome: saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, fecal incontinence, bilateral motor deficit.
  • Progressive neurological deficit: worsening of muscle strength in less than 24-48 hours.
  • Suspicion of infection: fever, severe low back pain, leukocytosis or factors risk (diabetes mellitus, immunosuppression).
  • Suspected neoplasia: history of cancer, weight loss, night pain, age >50 years without improvement.
  • Recent trauma with fracture or instability, or associated neurological deficit.
  • Non-contrast MRI is sufficient for herniated disc; Contrast is added if tumor or infection is suspected.

items.title: Cauda equina syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Caquina equina syndrome

  • Caquina equina syndrome: surgical emergency caused by compression of the lumbar and sacral roots due to massive herniated disc, tumor or hematoma.
  • Clinical: saddle anesthesia (loss of sensitivity in the perineal region), urinary retention with overflow incontinence, fecal incontinence and bilateral weakness of the lower limbs.
  • Examination: loss of tone of the anal sphincter, absence of bulbo-cavernous reflex and decreased perineal sensitivity.
  • Diagnosis: urgent magnetic resonance imaging; Surgical decompression should be performed within <48 hours to avoid irreversible damage.
  • Prognosis: Recovery of bladder function is incomplete if surgery is delayed more than 48 hours.

items.title: Initial conservative treatment
items.contentHtml:
Initial conservative treatment

  • First line: analgesics such as paracetamol (up to 4 g/day) or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen 400-800 mg every 8 hours) for 1-2 weeks.
  • Relative rest for 1-2 days, avoiding prolonged absolute rest that worsens muscle function and delays recovery.
  • Physiotherapy with core strengthening exercises and stretching; early mobilization reduces chronicity.
  • Add muscle relaxants (benzodiazepine cycle or baclofen) only if there is severe muscle spasm, for the short term.
  • Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 60 mg/day for 5 days) can be used in acute radicular pain, but without solid evidence; Epidural infiltration is reserved for refractory cases.

items.title: Surgical indications for herniated disc
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indications for herniated disc

  • Caquina equina syndrome: urgent surgery in <48 hours.
  • Progressive motor deficit or severe (muscle strength <3/5) that does not improve with conservative treatment in 4-6 weeks.
  • Disabling radicular pain that persists after 6-8 weeks of optimal conservative management and affects quality of life.
  • Cervical disc herniation with myelopathy (Babinski signs, hyperreflexia, clonus): requires early surgical decompression.
  • The standard technique is microdiscectomy; in selected cases, endoscopic discectomy with shorter recovery time.
  • Relative contraindications: severe comorbidities, psychiatric instability or unrealistic expectations.

items.title: Cervical disc herniation and myelopathy
items.contentHtml:
Cervical disc herniation and myelopathy

  • The most common cervical disc herniation is at the C5-C6 and C6-C7 level, producing radiculopathy with pain in the corresponding dermatome (C6: thumb; C7: middle finger).
  • Cervical myelopathy: spinal cord compression that causes hyperreflexia, clonus, sign of Babinski, loss of vibratory sensitivity and weakness in the lower limbs.
  • The Lhermitte maneuver (sensation of electric shock when flexing the neck) is typical of cervical spinal cord compression.
  • Cervical magnetic resonance imaging is the study of choice; Surgery (anterior discectomy with fusion or arthroplasty) is indicated in progressive myelopathy or motor deficit.
  • Untreated cervical myelopathy can progress to irreversible paraplegia.

items.title: Osteotendinous reflexes and their alteration
items.contentHtml:
Ostetendon reflexes and their alteration

  • Patellar reflex (L4): it is explored by percussing the patellar tendon; its absence or decrease indicates injury to the L4 root.
  • Achilles reflex (S1): striking the Achilles tendon; Its abolition leads to compression of the S1 root.
  • There is no specific osteotendinous reflex for the L5 root; motor alteration (dorsiflexion of the first toe) is key in its evaluation.
  • Hyperreflexia (vivid reflexes, clonus) suggests upper motor neuron injury (cervical myelopathy or multiple sclerosis), not lumbar disc herniation.
  • Bilateral comparison is essential; The asymmetry is more significant than the bilateral abolition that may be physiological in the elderly.

items.title: Muscle strength test in sciatica
items.contentHtml:
Muscle strength test in sciatica

  • L4 root: knee extension (against the quadriceps); strength evaluated on a 0-5 Medical Research Council (MRC) scale.
  • L5 Root: dorsiflexion of the first toe (extensor hallucis longus) and dorsiflexion of the foot (tibialis anterior); weakness of the first toe is the earliest finding.
  • Root S1: plantar flexion of the foot (gastrocnemius and soleus); It is evaluated by asking the patient to stand on tiptoe; weakness limits walking.
  • The heel walk test (L5) and toe walk (S1) are quick in-office screening tools.
  • A motor deficit <3/5 (movement against gravity impossible) is an indication for surgery if it persists after 4-6 weeks of conservative treatment.

items.foco: Risk factors and mechanisms of injury
items.title: Risk factors and mechanisms of injury
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors and mechanisms of injury

  • Mechanism: herniated disc is caused by degeneration of the nucleus pulposus that protrudes through the annulus fibrosus, most frequently due to repetitive lumbar flexion and rotation.
  • Risk factors: age (peak between 30-50 years), smoking (disk ischemia), occupation with lifting of loads and vibration, obesity (increased intradiscal pressure).
  • Genetic predisposition influences disc degeneration; certain type IX collagen polymorphisms are associated with a higher risk.
  • Acute trauma (fall, car accident) can precipitate herniation in a previously degenerated disc.
  • Sedentary lifestyle weakens the paraspinal musculature, reducing the protection of the disc against mechanical loads.
